additionalTime: 48 to 72 hours dough fermentation plus 3 hours dough warm-up
author: Robert Carr
description: A large 15 1/2- to 16-inch New York-style vodka pepperoni pizza built for a 16 x 16-inch baking steel, with a 72-hour high-gluten crust, thick vodka tomato sauce, whole-milk mozzarella, provolone, Pecorino Romano, cup-and-char pepperoni, pickled hot cherry peppers, basil, and a controlled broiler finish.
equipment: Covered dough container or large zip-top bag
Microwave-safe bowl, optional for warming the rim oil
Room-temperature rimless baking sheet, for finishing if the bottom crust browns early

groceryIngredients.display: high-gluten or high-protein bread flour, preferably King Arthur Sir Lancelot, Central Milling, or another strong bread flour
72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix pack, optional dough shortcut
groceryIngredients.note: Buy one pack if using instead of scratch dough; add 1 3/4 cups water to the mix, ferment as directed, then portion 500 to 525 grams prepared dough for one large 15 1/2- to 16-inch pizza.

groceryIngredients.display: fine sea salt, Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, or Diamond Crystal kosher salt
San Marzano-style or Bianco DiNapoli whole peeled tomatoes
groceryIngredients.note: Buy one 14-ounce can; the recipe uses 2/3 cup thick crushed drained tomatoes.

groceryIngredients.display: Calabrian chile paste or crushed red pepper flakes
groceryIngredients.note: The recipe uses 1/4 teaspoon for the sauce. Use a small pinch crushed red pepper flakes if you do not have Calabrian chile paste.

groceryIngredients.display: low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella block, preferably Grande, Galbani, Boar's Head, or another whole-milk block
sharp deli provolone or provolone piccante

groceryIngredients.note: Buy one small jar; the recipe uses 2 to 3 tablespoons drained and patted dry.

ingredients: 320 grams high-gluten or high-protein bread flour, preferably King Arthur Sir Lancelot, Central Milling, or another strong bread flour
198 grams cool water, about 3/4 cup plus 1 tablespoon plus 1 teaspoon
6 grams granulated sugar for the dough, about 1 1/2 teaspoons
Scant 1/8 teaspoon instant yeast, about 0.4 grams
8 grams salt for the dough: about 1 1/2 level teaspoons fine sea salt, 1 3/4 level teaspoons Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, or 2 1/2 level teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt
2 teaspoons extra-virgin olive oil for the dough, plus 1 teaspoon for the dough container
1 to 2 tablespoons semolina flour or fine cornmeal, for dusting the peel
Optional dough shortcut: 500 to 525 grams prepared dough from 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix for one large 15 1/2- to 16-inch pizza
Optional ready-made dough shortcut: 500 to 525 grams fresh cold-fermented New York-style or deck-oven pizzeria dough
1 (14-ounce) can San Marzano-style or Bianco DiNapoli whole peeled tomatoes, using 2/3 cup thick crushed drained tomatoes
1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil for the sauce
1 tablespoon unsalted butter for the sauce
2 tablespoons finely minced shallot, from about 1 small shallot
2 small garlic cloves, finely grated for the sauce
1 tablespoon tomato paste
1/4 cup vodka
1/2 teaspoon dried oregano for the sauce
1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt for the sauce, plus more to taste
1/4 teaspoon Calabrian chile paste for the sauce, or a small pinch crushed red pepper flakes
2 tablespoons heavy cream
1 tablespoon finely grated Pecorino Romano for the sauce
Small pinch granulated sugar, only if the tomatoes taste sharp
6 1/2 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block for a 15 1/2-inch pizza, or 7 1/2 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza, freshly shredded
1 1/2 ounces sharp deli provolone or provolone piccante, finely diced or shredded
1 1/4 ounces Pecorino Romano, finely grated, divided
3 1/2 ounces natural-casing cup-and-char pepperoni for a 15 1/2-inch pizza, or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza, sliced thin
2 tablespoons drained sliced pickled hot cherry peppers for a 15 1/2-inch pizza, or 3 tablespoons for a true 16-inch pizza, patted dry
1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil for the garlic-oregano rim oil
1 small garlic clove, finely grated for the garlic-oregano rim oil
1/8 teaspoon dried oregano for the garlic-oregano rim oil
1/8 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible
10 to 12 fresh basil leaves, torn after baking
Small pinch flaky sea salt for finishing, optional
instructions: Using 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix? Skip to step 13. Using 500 to 525 grams ready-made cold-fermented pizzeria dough? Skip to step 15.

Two to three days before baking, make the scratch dough by whisking 198 grams cool water, 6 grams granulated sugar, and 8 grams salt in a large mixing bowl until the sugar dissolves and the salt is mostly dissolved.
Whisk the scant 1/8 teaspoon instant yeast, about 0.4 grams, into the salted sugar water.
Add all 320 grams high-gluten or high-protein bread flour to the bowl with the yeasted water.
Mix with a dough scraper, spatula, or your hand for 1 to 2 minutes, until no dry flour remains and the dough looks shaggy.
Cover the bowl and rest the dough for 20 minutes so the flour hydrates before kneading.
Drizzle 2 teaspoons extra-virgin olive oil over the dough.
Knead or fold the dough in the bowl for 3 to 4 minutes, until the oil is absorbed and the dough looks smoother but still slightly tacky.
Cover the bowl again and rest the dough at room temperature for 30 minutes.
With wet hands, stretch one side of the dough up and fold it over the center, then rotate the bowl and repeat 3 more times so all 4 sides have been folded.
Shape the dough into a tight ball, lightly coat a covered dough container or large zip-top bag with 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, and place the dough inside.
Cover the dough and refrigerate it for 48 to 72 hours, using the full 72 hours when possible for deeper crust flavor and easier stretching.

If using the dough mix instead of the scratch dough, pour the mix into a bowl, add 1 3/4 cups water, knead for 2 to 3 minutes, rest the covered dough at room temperature for 12 to 24 hours, then refrigerate the covered dough for 2 days.
For the dough mix, portion 500 to 525 grams prepared dough about 3 hours before baking, shape that portion into a smooth ball, and save the remaining dough for another pizza or bread.
About 3 hours before baking, remove the cold scratch dough, dough-mix portion, or ready-made pizzeria dough from the refrigerator.
If the dough has spread in the container, gently tighten it back into a smooth ball without heavily kneading or degassing it.

Place the dough ball in a lightly oiled bowl or container, cover it, and let it warm at room temperature for about 3 hours, until relaxed, slightly puffy, and no longer refrigerator-cold in the center.
Place the baking steel on the second-highest rack, ideally about 5 to 6 inches below the broiler element.
Preheat the oven to 550 degrees F for 60 to 90 minutes so the baking steel is fully saturated with heat before the pizza goes in.
While the steel preheats, spoon the tomatoes from the can into a fine-mesh strainer and let watery juice drain for 10 minutes.
Crush enough drained tomatoes by hand to measure 2/3 cup thick crushed drained tomatoes.
Make the vodka sauce by warming 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil and 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in a small saucepan over medium-low heat until the butter melts.
Add 2 tablespoons finely minced shallot to the saucepan and cook for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until the shallot is softened but not browned.

Add 2 finely grated small garlic cloves, 1 tablespoon tomato paste, 1/2 teaspoon dried oregano, and 1/4 teaspoon Calabrian chile paste or a small pinch crushed red pepper flakes to the shallot mixture.
Cook the tomato paste mixture for 30 to 45 seconds, stirring constantly, until it smells savory and the tomato paste darkens slightly.
Add 1/4 cup vodka to the saucepan and simmer for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until the vodka is reduced to about 1 tablespoon and the raw alcohol smell has softened.
Stir the 2/3 cup thick crushed drained tomatoes and 1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt into the reduced vodka mixture.
Simmer the sauce for 4 to 6 minutes, stirring often, until it is thick enough to leave a path when a spoon is dragged across the bottom of the saucepan.
Turn off the heat and stir 2 tablespoons heavy cream and 1 tablespoon finely grated Pecorino Romano into the thick tomato sauce.
Taste the vodka sauce; it should be bright, savory, creamy, and slightly salty because it has to season a large crust, cheese, pepperoni, and cherry peppers.
If the vodka sauce tastes flat, stir in another tiny pinch of fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, about 1/16 teaspoon; if it tastes sharp, stir in a small pinch of granulated sugar.
Transfer the sauce to a small bowl and let it cool until warm or room temperature before topping the pizza so it does not soften the stretched dough.
Freshly shred 6 1/2 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella for a 15 1/2-inch pizza, or 7 1/2 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza.
Finely dice or shred 1 1/2 ounces sharp deli provolone or provolone piccante.
Toss together the measured mozzarella, 1 1/2 ounces provolone, and 1/2 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano so the cheese blend distributes evenly.
Keep the cheese blend cold until assembly so it melts cleanly instead of oiling out on the peel.
Slice 3 1/2 ounces pepperoni for a 15 1/2-inch pizza or 4 ounces pepperoni for a true 16-inch pizza, unless the pepperoni is already sliced thin.

Drain 2 tablespoons sliced pickled hot cherry peppers for a 15 1/2-inch pizza or 3 tablespoons for a true 16-inch pizza, then pat the peppers dry with paper towels so their brine does not soften the center crust.

Make the garlic-oregano rim oil by warming 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil and 1 finely grated small garlic clove in a small saucepan over very low heat or in a microwave-safe bowl for 10 to 15 seconds, just until warm and fragrant.
Stir 1/8 teaspoon dried oregano and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper into the warm garlic oil, then set it aside until the pizza is baked.
Dust a 16-inch pizza peel with 1 tablespoon semolina flour or fine cornmeal.
Lightly flour the counter, place the room-temperature dough on the counter, and press from the center outward with your fingertips, leaving the outer 3/4 inch untouched and slightly puffy.
Stretch the dough into a 15 1/2- to 16-inch round, keeping the center thin and the rim just slightly thicker than the center.
If the dough springs back while stretching, cover it and rest it for 5 minutes, then continue stretching.
Transfer the stretched dough to the semolina-dusted peel and gently shake the peel once to confirm the dough slides.
If any part sticks to the peel, lift that edge and dust a little more semolina flour or fine cornmeal underneath before topping.
Scatter about 2 ounces of the cold cheese blend directly over the stretched dough in a very thin layer, focusing on the center and leaving a 3/4-inch rim uncovered.
Spread all of the cooled vodka sauce, about 2/3 to 3/4 cup, over the thin cheese layer in an even layer, leaving the 3/4-inch rim uncovered.
Scatter the remaining cheese blend evenly over the vodka sauce.
Arrange 3 1/2 ounces sliced pepperoni over a 15 1/2-inch pizza or 4 ounces over a true 16-inch pizza, leaving a little space between slices so the cheese can bubble through.
Scatter 2 tablespoons drained and patted-dry pickled hot cherry peppers over a 15 1/2-inch pizza or 3 tablespoons over a true 16-inch pizza, avoiding heavy piles in the center.
Sprinkle 1/4 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano over the topped pizza before baking.
Gently shake the peel again before launching the topped pizza; the pizza should slide freely as one piece.
If any spot sticks after topping, lift that edge with floured fingers or a bench scraper and dust a small pinch of semolina flour or fine cornmeal underneath.
Launch the pizza onto the fully preheated baking steel as soon as it slides freely, ideally within 2 minutes of adding sauce, so moisture from the toppings does not glue the dough to the peel.
Bake the pizza at 550 degrees F for 3 minutes with the broiler off.
At 3 minutes, rotate the pizza 180 degrees with the peel so the back edge does not brown faster than the front edge.
Check the bottom crust by lifting one edge with the peel; at 3 minutes it should be set and lightly browned, not floppy, dark brown, or black.
If the bottom is still pale and flexible at 3 minutes, leave the pizza directly on the steel for 45 to 60 seconds more before using the broiler.
If the bottom already has dark brown spots at 3 minutes, slide the pizza onto a room-temperature rimless baking sheet before continuing so the bottom stops darkening on the steel.
Turn the broiler to high and broil for 30 to 75 seconds, watching constantly, until the rim blisters, the cheese bubbles, and the pepperoni cups lightly crisp without blackening.
If the rim or pepperoni darkens before the cheese is fully bubbling, turn the broiler off and finish at 550 degrees F for 30 to 90 seconds.

If the top still needs more color after 75 seconds under the broiler, turn the broiler off, return the oven to 550 degrees F, and bake for 1 to 2 minutes more while checking the bottom every 30 seconds.
If the bottom becomes deep golden before the top is finished, move the pizza onto the room-temperature rimless baking sheet and finish the top under the broiler for 30 to 60 seconds.
Transfer the baked pizza to a cutting board and let it rest for 2 minutes so the cheese settles and the crust stays crisp when sliced.
Brush only the exposed outer rim with about 2 teaspoons garlic-oregano rim oil while the crust is still hot; avoid brushing the center so the pizza does not turn greasy.
Sprinkle the remaining 1/2 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano over the pizza.
Tear 10 to 12 fresh basil leaves over the pizza and add a small pinch of flaky sea salt if using.
Slice the pizza and serve immediately while the bottom crust is crisp, the center is foldable, the rim is tender, and the pepperoni oil is still glossy.
notes: For deeper crust flavor and more control, use the scratch dough and cold-ferment it for the full 72 hours. The dough uses sugar and oil for New York-style tenderness and browning, but the amounts are kept moderate so the steel at 550 degrees F does not scorch the bottom before the top finishes.

The scratch dough makes about one 535-gram dough ball, enough for a large 15 1/2- to 16-inch pizza with a crisp bottom, foldable center, and a rim that has enough structure for vodka sauce and pepperoni.

If using a mix, 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix works well here because it is designed for a long ferment and for the same steel-focused baking style. Use 500 grams prepared dough for a 15 1/2-inch pizza or 525 grams for a true 16-inch pizza.

If buying ready-made dough instead, use 500 to 525 grams of fresh cold-fermented New York-style or deck-oven dough from a local pizzeria, Italian market, or bakery. Ask for dough made with strong bread flour or high-gluten flour. Avoid canned dough, par-baked crusts, very sweet grocery-store dough, oily dough, or dough that tears immediately when stretched.

For the first bake, use about 500 grams dough stretched to 15 1/2 inches with the lower pepperoni and cherry pepper amounts. Use 525 grams dough, the full 16-inch stretch, and the higher topping amounts once you are comfortable with the launch and bottom color.
A true 16-inch pizza leaves almost no launch margin on a 16 x 16-inch steel. Stretch to 15 1/2 inches the first time if you are not completely comfortable launching large pies.
Use a 16-inch peel for this pizza. A smaller peel makes a 15 1/2- to 16-inch dough difficult to launch cleanly.
Do not use parchment paper at 550 degrees F or under the broiler. A semolina-dusted peel gives a clean launch and avoids scorched paper edges.
Preheating the steel for 60 to 90 minutes matters because thick steel needs time to heat through. A short preheat can leave the top cooked before the bottom crisps.
The broiler is only for finishing. Watch constantly once the broiler is on because pepperoni, cheese, and the rim can go from blistered to burned quickly.
Use the room-temperature rimless baking sheet if the bottom browns faster than the top. It lets the top finish without more direct steel contact.
Keep the vodka sauce thick, cooled, and measured. Too much sauce or warm sauce can soften the center before the crust sets.
The thin cheese layer under the vodka sauce helps protect the center crust from the creamy tomato sauce while still keeping the pizza sauce-forward.

Use low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block because it melts well without flooding the pizza. Grande, Galbani, Boar's Head, or another whole-milk block will melt more cleanly than pre-shredded mozzarella.
Sharp provolone adds the slice-shop edge without making the center greasy. Keep it to 1 1/2 ounces so the cheese blend stays balanced.
Natural-casing cup-and-char pepperoni adds savory depth because it curls, crisps at the edges, and releases seasoned oil into the cheese.
Pat the pickled cherry peppers dry before topping the pizza. Their acidity is important to balance the vodka sauce and pepperoni, but excess brine can soften the crust.
Brush the rim with garlic-oregano oil after baking, not before baking. Post-bake oil seasons the crust and adds aroma without encouraging the rim to burn.
If the dough resists stretching, rest it for 5 minutes. Forcing tight dough knocks out gas and can make the rim dense.

If the bottom is too dark before the top is browned, use the room-temperature sheet sooner during that bake; next time, either move the steel one rack lower or start checking the bottom at 2 1/2 minutes.
If the top browns before the bottom crisps, keep the broiler off longer; next time, preheat the steel for the full 90 minutes and bake 1 rack position lower before using the broiler.
If the center is soft, drain the tomatoes longer, cool the sauce fully, pat the cherry peppers drier, and use 1 to 2 tablespoons less sauce next time.
For a milder pizza, omit the Calabrian chile paste and reduce the pickled cherry peppers to 1 tablespoon. Keep the pepperoni amount the same so the pizza still has enough savory depth.
title: New York Style Vodka Pepperoni Pizza
totalTime: 1 hour 20 minutes active, plus 48 to 72 hours dough fermentation and 3 hours dough warm-up
